content.text: Automated static analysis transforms raw Python into consistent, bug-resistant code before a single test runs. In agentic AI codebases — where tool handlers, orchestrators, and eval harnesses grow quickly and sometimes resemble generated boilerplate — linting catches real bugs early and formatting eliminates style debates so every code review focuses on agent logic, safety, and correctness rather than indentation.

performs static analysis: it parses source code into an AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) and checks it against a rule set without executing it. Rules range from syntactic style (trailing whitespace, max line length) to semantic footguns (undefined variable names, bare
goes further: it rewrites the file in-place to a canonical layout. The result is idempotent — running a formatter twice produces the same output.
The dominant Python tool in 2024–2025 is

, written in Rust by Astral. Ruff bundles both a linter (with ≥ 800 rules drawn from pyflakes, pycodestyle, isort, flake8-bugbear, and more) and a formatter that is intentionally compatible with Black's output. A single dependency replaces a stack of tools and runs 10–100× faster than the Python equivalents.
remains popular as a standalone formatter, especially in projects that adopted it before Ruff reached formatter maturity.

For JavaScript/TypeScript agents (LangChain.js, Vercel AI SDK, custom MCP servers), ESLint handles linting and Prettier handles formatting. Both ecosystems follow the same architectural pattern: static analysis at the file level, integrated into editors, pre-commit hooks, and CI.
Agent codebases have several properties that amplify the value of automated static analysis compared with ordinary application code.
. Orchestrators that call LLMs, execute tools, and fan out sub-tasks all run inside async event loops. A single synchronous
blocks the event loop and stalls every concurrent tool call. The Ruff
configurations) catches these at lint time.
tool handler registries use mutable defaults
— the list is shared across all calls, silently accumulating registrations. The
bugbear rules flag this pattern immediately.

. Eval scripts, red-teaming harnesses, and synthetic data generators are often written quickly. Without a formatter, each contributor uses personal preferences, producing diffs that mix style noise with logical changes. Reviewers cannot tell at a glance which lines actually matter.

Finally, consistent style acts as a readability multiplier for AI coding assistants. When the entire codebase follows one style, model-generated edits land cleanly without fighting formatter opinions, reducing the manual cleanup cycle.
— Undefined names, unused variables, import cycles, and mutable defaults are caught before tests run.
— Formatting before commit eliminates whitespace-only lines from diffs; reviewers see only substantive changes.
— Ruff processes 100 000-line repos in under 0.5 seconds on a laptop, making pre-commit hooks invisible.
— ruff check --fix and ruff format --fix remove the manual step for the majority of violations.
— Ruff replaces Black, isort, pyflakes, pycodestyle, and flake8-bugbear. One tool, one version pin, one config section.
— VS Code, Neovim, and PyCharm all have first-class Ruff plugins; format-on-save gives instant feedback without leaving the editor.
— New contributors run one command and their environment is code-style-compliant from day one.
— The default Ruff rule set is large. Teams need a deliberate decision about which rule groups to enable; overly strict rules frustrate contributors who then add noqa comments indiscriminately.

— Introducing a formatter into an existing codebase produces a single massive diff that poisons git blame. Strategies: use --diff first, pick a dedicated 'format' commit, exclude lines from blame with .git-blame-ignore-revs.
— A linter cannot assess whether agent logic is correct. It will not warn that a tool handler calls the wrong API or that a prompt template produces hallucinations.
— Prompt files in Markdown, tool schemas in JSON/YAML, and OpenAPI specs require different linters (markdownlint, jsonschema, Spectral). Ruff ignores these files.
— Black/Ruff use double quotes; teams using single quotes must accept the change or configure the formatter, not fight it.
— Even Ruff at 50ms for a standard repo becomes 5s+ on a 10 million-line monorepo; CI sharding becomes necessary.
Ruff parses Python source into a concrete syntax tree (CST) using
, then runs each enabled rule as a visitor over the tree. Rules report
), message, source span, and optionally a
object describing the minimal text edit needed to resolve the violation. When
is passed, Ruff applies all non-conflicting fixes, sorts them by offset, and rewrites the file atomically.

Ruff's linter operates in two modes. In check mode it exits with a non-zero status if any violations remain after auto-fix (useful for CI). In watch mode (ruff check --watch) it monitors the file system and re-checks changed files, providing sub-100ms feedback loops during development.

layout algorithm (also used by Prettier and rustfmt) to render the CST into formatted text. The algorithm treats formatting as a document algebra: it tries to fit an expression on one line; if it exceeds
, it expands to the multi-line form. This produces stable, deterministic output — the same source always formats the same way regardless of who edited it or when.

Unlike a linter, the formatter does not operate on a rule set. There is no config knob that says 'trailing comma style'. The formatter makes one opinionated choice and applies it everywhere. This is deliberate: it eliminates configuration-as-bikeshedding and makes the formatter fast to adopt.
rule group in Ruff implements isort semantics: standard-library imports come first, then third-party packages, then local modules, each group separated by a blank line. Ruff's isort is aware of
imports. In agent codebases that import heavily from
modules, consistent import ordering is essential for rapid scanning.

The diagram below shows the full linting and formatting pipeline from developer keystrokes to merged commit, including the two feedback loops (pre-commit rejection and CI gate failure) that enforce the policy at every stage.

attrs.alt: Linting & Formatting pipeline for agentic AI codebases — editor, pre-commit, CI gate

content.text: The pipeline has four checkpoints: the editor (format-on-save), the pre-commit hook (lint + format on staged files), the CI gate (lint + format --check on the full repo), and the merge policy (PRs blocked on CI failure). All four share the same pyproject.toml configuration to guarantee identical behaviour across environments.

Linting and formatting should be active from the first commit of every Python agent project. There is no meaningful cost to introducing them early; the cost of introducing them late is a big-bang formatting commit that pollutes git history.
Enable the following rule groups immediately:
— baseline style; catches obvious formatting issues before the formatter.
— undefined names, unused imports; catches real bugs, not just style.
— import ordering; near-zero false positives, auto-fixable.
— mutable defaults, assert in non-test code, bare except; highly relevant to tool handler code.
— modernise syntax to the target Python version; purely stylistic, auto-fixable.
Add incrementally as the team grows comfortable:
— blocking calls in async functions; critical once the codebase uses asyncio for tool execution.
— security rules: hardcoded passwords, shell injection; relevant to eval harnesses.
— class/function naming conventions; useful for agent SDK design.
on generated protobuf/thrift stubs, vendored third-party code, or auto-generated ORM migrations. Exclude these paths explicitly in
rather than polluting the source tree with

# Add to project dev dependencies
pip install ruff
# Or with uv (recommended for agent projects)
uv add --dev ruff

[tool.ruff]
line-length = 100
target-version = "py312"
# Exclude generated/vendored paths
exclude = [
    ".git",
    ".venv",
    "dist",
    "build",
    "migrations",          # auto-generated DB migrations
    "src/generated",      # protobuf stubs
]

[tool.ruff.lint]
# Enable rule groups
select = [
    "E",      # pycodestyle errors
    "W",      # pycodestyle warnings
    "F",      # pyflakes
    "I",      # isort
    "B",      # flake8-bugbear
    "UP",     # pyupgrade
    "ASYNC",  # flake8-async (blocking calls in async)
]
ignore = [
    "E501",   # line too long — formatter handles this
    "B008",   # do not perform function calls in default args (allow FastAPI Depends)
]
# Allow auto-fix for these rules
fixable = ["ALL"]
unfixable = []

[tool.ruff.lint.isort]
known-first-party = ["agents", "tools", "evals"]

[tool.ruff.format]
quote-style = "double"
indent-style = "space"
skip-magic-trailing-comma = false
line-ending = "auto"

# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit
    rev: v0.4.9   # pin to a specific version for reproducibility
    hooks:
      - id: ruff
        args: [--fix, --exit-non-zero-on-fix]
        types_or: [python, pyi]
      - id: ruff-format
        types_or: [python, pyi]

# Install pre-commit and activate hooks
pip install pre-commit
pre-commit install

# Run manually on all files (e.g. when first adding to an existing repo)
pre-commit run --all-files

# .github/workflows/lint.yml
name: Lint & Format

on:
  pull_request:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  ruff:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: astral-sh/ruff-action@v1
        with:
          args: check . --output-format=github
      - uses: astral-sh/ruff-action@v1
        with:
          args: format --check .
Example 1 — Simple: detecting and fixing common agent bugs
The following file demonstrates five common issues that Ruff flags in agent code. Run
ruff check --fix agent_bad.py && ruff format agent_bad.py

# agent_bad.py — deliberately broken; run ruff check to see violations
import os
import asyncio
import json       # F401: imported but unused
import requests  # used below, but in an async context — ASYNC100
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

# B006: mutable default argument — shared across ALL calls
def register_tool(name: str, tools: list = []):
    tools.append(name)
    return tools

# ASYNC100: blocking HTTP call inside async function
async def fetch_context(url: str) -> dict:
    response = requests.get(url)   # blocks the event loop!
    return response.json()

# B001 / B002: bare except catches everything including SystemExit
async def run_agent(prompt: str):
    try:
        message = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
        )
        return message.content[0].text
    except:  # B001: do not use bare except
        return None

# Output of: ruff check agent_bad.py
# agent_bad.py:3:1: F401 [*] `json` imported but unused
# agent_bad.py:12:24: B006 Do not use mutable data structures for argument defaults
# agent_bad.py:18:5: ASYNC100 [*] Async functions should not call blocking HTTP methods
# agent_bad.py:28:5: B001 Do not use bare `except`, use `except Exception:`
# Found 4 errors.

# agent_good.py — fixed version
import asyncio

import httpx  # async-native HTTP client
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()


# Fixed: mutable default replaced with None sentinel
def register_tool(name: str, tools: list | None = None) -> list:
    if tools is None:
        tools = []
    tools.append(name)
    return tools


# Fixed: non-blocking HTTP call
async def fetch_context(url: str) -> dict:
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as http:
        response = await http.get(url)
        response.raise_for_status()
        return response.json()


# Fixed: specific exception type
async def run_agent(prompt: str) -> str | None:
    try:
        message = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        )
        return message.content[0].text
    except Exception as exc:
        print(f"Agent call failed: {exc}")
        return None
Example 2 — Realistic: full CI-ready agent project setup
The following shows a production-style agent project with a Makefile-driven workflow that mirrors what the CI does, so developers never see CI-only failures.

# tools/search.py — an example agent tool (shows import style that ruff enforces)
from __future__ import annotations

import asyncio
import os
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any

import httpx

from agents.base import BaseTool, ToolResult
from agents.config import settings


@dataclass
class SearchTool(BaseTool):
    """Async web search tool for the research agent."""

    name: str = "search"
    # Correct: no mutable default — dataclass handles this
    results: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list)
    _client: httpx.AsyncClient | None = field(default=None, init=False, repr=False)

    async def __aenter__(self) -> "SearchTool":
        self._client = httpx.AsyncClient(
            base_url=settings.search_api_base_url,
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['SEARCH_API_KEY']}"},
            timeout=10.0,
        )
        return self

    async def __aexit__(self, *args: Any) -> None:
        if self._client:
            await self._client.aclose()

    async def run(self, query: str, *, max_results: int = 5) -> ToolResult:
        """Execute a search query and return structured results."""
        if not self._client:
            msg = "SearchTool must be used as an async context manager"
            raise RuntimeError(msg)

        response = await self._client.get(
            "/search",
            params={"q": query, "limit": max_results},
        )
        response.raise_for_status()
        data = response.json()
        self.results = data.get("results", [])
        return ToolResult(content=self.results, tool_name=self.name)

# Makefile — single source of truth for lint/format commands
.PHONY: lint format check fix

lint:
	ruff check .

format:
	ruff format .

check:   ## What CI runs — exits non-zero if anything is wrong
	ruff check . --output-format=github
	ruff format --check .

fix:     ## Apply all safe auto-fixes then format
	ruff check --fix .
	ruff format .

# Run before pushing
pre-push: fix check
A team building a multi-agent research platform (orchestrator + 12 specialist sub-agents + shared tool library) adopts Ruff at the start of the project with 10 000 lines of Python.
to audit the initial state. The output shows 847 violations across F, I, B, and UP. They spend 30 minutes enabling
, resolving 821 automatically. The remaining 26 require manual review: 18 are
mutable defaults in tool registries (each needs a None-sentinel pattern), 5 are
bare excepts in legacy eval scripts.

After the cleanup commit, CI times improve. The previous CI ran flake8 (8s), isort (3s), and Black (4s) sequentially. Ruff replaces all three in 0.4s. The pre-commit hook goes from 15s to 2s on staged files, removing a major friction point for frequent committers.
Six months in, the repo has grown to 80 000 lines. A new engineer joins, clones the repo, installs pre-commit, and opens a file. Their editor shows

violations immediately via the VS Code extension. They fix the violations before their first commit. The team's code reviews contain zero style comments; every comment is about agent architecture, safety, or correctness.
Running lint only in CI, not locally
Without a pre-commit hook, developers discover violations only after pushing. The feedback loop is minutes instead of seconds. Fix: install
as part of the project setup script.
. Lint passes but formatting diverges. Every developer's PR has spurious style changes. Fix: both commands must be in CI, and both must fail the build.

# Wrong — suppresses entire line, hides real issues later
result = eval(user_input)  # noqa

# Right — specific code, reason as comment
result = eval(user_input)  # noqa: S307  # prompt sanitized upstream, eval required for dynamic agent DSL
Excluding hand-written code as if it were generated
to work around a large violation count on introduction. This defeats the purpose. Instead: run
to auto-fix the majority and manually fix the remainder. The one-time cost is worth it.
Mismatched tool versions between local and CI
in CI while a developer has
locally causes formatting differences. Fix: pin
and mirror the same version in
— put all Ruff settings in pyproject.toml [tool.ruff]. Never scatter .ruff.toml, setup.cfg, and .flake8 fragments across the repo.
Enable autofix in pre-commit, not in CI
— pre-commit runs ruff check --fix to help developers; CI runs ruff check (no --fix) so violations fail loudly rather than being silently corrected.
Separate format check from lint check in CI
— ruff check and ruff format --check are two distinct steps. One catching violations while the other does not is a misconfiguration, not a partial pass.
— this ensures that when ruff auto-fixes a file, git pauses to let the developer review and re-stage the fixed file rather than committing the unfixed version.
Add .git-blame-ignore-revs after a big format commit
— create a .git-blame-ignore-revs file listing the SHA of the format-only commit; git blame --ignore-revs-file .git-blame-ignore-revs then skips it.
Lint YAML tool schemas with Spectral or jsonschema
— MCP tool definitions and OpenAPI specs are not Python; lint them in a separate CI step alongside ruff.
— add ruff check --statistics | grep noqa to CI output. A rising noqa count signals rule-suppression creep.

Ruff's Rust implementation delivers a qualitatively different performance profile compared with Python-based tools. Benchmarks from the Astral team show Ruff checking CPython (560 000 lines) in 0.29 seconds versus flake8's 15 seconds — roughly 50× faster.

For most agent projects (10 000–100 000 lines), Ruff's check pass completes in under 500ms on a developer laptop, making pre-commit hooks transparent. At 1 million lines (a large monorepo), a full check takes 3–5 seconds, still reasonable for pre-push but potentially slow for pre-commit. At that scale, configure pre-commit to run on staged files only (the default) and reserve full-repo checks for CI.
Ruff automatically uses all available CPU cores during the check pass. On GitHub Actions

runners (2 vCPUs), a 500 000-line repo checks in under 2 seconds. For monorepos with multiple Python services, shard lint across services with a matrix strategy rather than running Ruff on the entire checkout.

# Parallel lint across services in a monorepo
jobs:
  lint:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        service: [orchestrator, tools, evals, api]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: astral-sh/ruff-action@v1
        with:
          src: ./${{ matrix.service }}
          args: check . --output-format=github
Ruff maintains a file-level cache in
. On subsequent runs it only re-checks files whose content has changed. Cache the
directory in CI with a key that includes the Ruff version and
hash. On a 100 000-line repo where 20 files changed in a PR, the cached run takes under 50ms.
— Ruff extension (astral-sh.ruff-vscode): lint-on-save, format-on-save, quick-fix actions. Enable with: editor.formatOnSave + [python] editor.defaultFormatter: charliermarsh.ruff.
— null-ls or nvim-lint + conform.nvim; or the native LSP via ruff-lsp.
— File Watcher plugin running ruff format on save; or the third-party Ruff plugin.
— flycheck-ruff for on-the-fly lint, reformatter.el for format-on-save.

# .markdownlint.yaml — lint Markdown prompt templates
default: true
MD013:  # line-length
  line_length: 200
  # prompts often have long lines
MD033: false  # allow inline HTML in prompts

# Lint an MCP tool definition JSON schema against the MCP spec
npx @stoplight/spectral-cli lint tools/search_tool.json --ruleset mcp-ruleset.yaml
The following comparison covers the main tools for Python linting and formatting:
Teams needing a mature, battle-tested formatter
Deep semantic checks; large enterprise repos
Standalone import sort; superseded by Ruff I rules
Type safety; complements, not replaces, linting
rule group (based on bandit) surfaces several security-relevant patterns that appear in agent codebases:
— Direct use of eval() on LLM output is a code-injection vector. Ruff flags every eval() call; each must be explicitly justified with a noqa comment.
— assert statements are stripped by Python when running with -O (optimized mode). Tool handler preconditions written as asserts silently disappear. Use explicit if/raise instead.
— API keys and secrets committed to source are flagged immediately, before they can be pushed.
— Shell injection via user-controlled input to subprocess.run(shell=True) is caught and flagged.
Pin the Ruff version in both

). A Ruff upgrade can change which rules fire or how code is formatted, producing spurious CI failures on unrelated PRs if versions drift. Upgrade intentionally with a dedicated PR that touches only the version pin and any resulting auto-fix changes.
Ruff writes formatted files atomically (write to a temp file, then rename) to prevent partial writes from corrupting source under concurrent tools. This is relevant when running
concurrently with a language server that is also reading those files.
Ruff combines a fast linter and a Black-compatible formatter in one Rust binary; it replaces flake8, isort, and Black with a single dependency and runs 10-50x faster.
Enable the F, I, B, and ASYNC rule groups immediately on every Python agent project — they catch real bugs (mutable defaults, blocking async calls, undefined names) not just style.
Pre-commit hooks (ruff check --fix + ruff format) give sub-second feedback locally; CI gates (ruff check + ruff format --check) enforce the policy on every PR.
All environments — editor plugin, pre-commit hook, CI — must share the same pyproject.toml configuration to guarantee identical behaviour and prevent CI-only surprises.
The S (bandit) rule group makes the linter a lightweight security layer, catching eval(), hardcoded secrets, and shell-injection patterns before they reach code review.
Introduce formatting to an existing repo with a single dedicated commit, then add that commit's SHA to .git-blame-ignore-revs so git blame remains useful.
Ruff does not replace type checking (mypy/pyright) or semantic correctness testing — it enforces code shape and catches shallow bugs; pair it with both type checking and agent-specific eval suites.
